title: Thumbelina
content:
There was once a woman who wished very much to have a little child, but she could not get her wish. At last she went to a fairy, and said, "I should so very much like to have a little child; can you tell me where I can find one?"

"Oh, that can be easily managed," said the fairy. "Here is a barley-corn. Put it into a flower-pot, and see what will happen."

The woman planted the barley-corn, and immediately there grew up a large handsome flower. The woman kissed the red and golden-colored leaves, and as she did so the flower opened. In the middle sat a very delicate and graceful little maiden, no bigger than a thumb, and so she was called Thumbelina.

A walnut-shell served her for a bed, blue violet-leaves were her mattress, and a rose-leaf was her blanket. At night she slept in her bed, and during the day she played on a table where the woman had placed a plate of water surrounded by flowers.

One night, as she lay in her pretty bed, an ugly toad crept through the window. The toad took her away to be a bride for her son.

"You shall marry my son," said the old toad.

But the little fishes in the stream felt sorry for Thumbelina. They nibbled through the stem of the lily-pad, and it floated away down the stream.

Thumbelina floated past many lands until a friendly swallow found her. "Winter is coming," said the swallow. "Come with me to the warm countries."

Thumbelina climbed on the swallow's back, and they flew over mountains and seas until they came to a beautiful land of sunshine.

There, among the flowers, lived tiny people just like Thumbelina. A lovely prince saw her and fell in love.

"Will you be my wife?" he asked. "You shall be queen of all the flowers."

Thumbelina said yes. The prince placed a pair of beautiful wings on her shoulders so she could fly from flower to flower.

And they lived happily ever after.

The End.

author: Hans Christian Andersen
